questions.question:
A virtual memory system uses First In First Out (FIFO) page replacement policy and allocates a fixed number of frames to a process. Consider the following statements:
P: Increasing the number of page frames allocated to a process sometimes increases the page fault rate.
Q: Some programs do not exhibit locality of reference.
Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. Both P and Q are true, and Q is the reason for P.
B. Both P and Q are true, and Q is not the reason for P.
C. P is false but Q is true.
D. Both P and Q are false.
questions.answer: B. Both P and Q are true, and Q is not the reason for P.
questions.to_find: Which statement about FIFO page replacement and page fault behavior is true

understand.plain: FIFO page replacement can suffer from Belady's anomaly, where adding more frames counterintuitively increases page faults. Some programs (e.g., with random access patterns) genuinely lack locality. Statement P is true because of Belady's anomaly, but Belady's anomaly occurs because of the non-stack property of FIFO, not because programs lack locality.

keywords.explain: A counterintuitive phenomenon in FIFO where increasing the number of page frames can increase the page fault rate instead of decreasing it.
keywords.example: With 3 frames reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5 has 9 faults; with 4 frames it has 10 faults.

keywords.explain: The tendency of a program to access the same set of memory locations repeatedly over a short time period (temporal and spatial locality).
keywords.example: A loop that reads array elements sequentially exhibits strong spatial locality.

understand.visual_alt: FIFO policy does not have the stack property, so adding frames can cause Belady's anomaly; locality of reference is independent of this phenomenon.

given.aim: Determine which of the two statements P and Q are true, and whether Q explains P.

terms.meaning: The oldest page in memory (first loaded) is replaced when a new page must be brought in.
terms.connects: FIFO lacks the stack property, making Belady's anomaly possible.

terms.meaning: Increasing frames sometimes increases page fault rate under FIFO.
terms.example: Classic Belady's anomaly example shows more faults with 4 frames than 3.
terms.connects: TRUE — this is the definition of Belady's anomaly which FIFO can exhibit.

terms.meaning: Some programs do not exhibit locality of reference.
terms.example: Programs with random memory access patterns lack locality.
terms.connects: TRUE — but Belady's anomaly is a property of FIFO, not caused by lack of locality.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement independently. P is true (Belady's anomaly exists for FIFO). Q is true (some programs like random-access programs lack locality). Then check causality: P is caused by FIFO's non-stack property, not by lack of locality — so Q is not the reason for P.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement P
steps.formula_raw: Belady's anomaly: FIFO can have more faults with more frames
steps.apply: FIFO does not satisfy the inclusion property (stack property). Therefore, with more frames, the set of pages resident is not always a superset of the set with fewer frames. This means page faults can increase as frames increase. P is TRUE.
steps.note: LRU and Optimal are stack algorithms and do NOT suffer Belady's anomaly.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement Q and the causal relationship
steps.apply: Q is TRUE: programs with random access patterns or irregular memory usage can lack spatial/temporal locality. However, Belady's anomaly (P) occurs because FIFO lacks the stack property — it is a mathematical property of the algorithm itself, independent of the program's locality behavior. Therefore Q does NOT cause P.
steps.note: The correct answer is B: both P and Q are true, but Q does not explain P.

solution.result: B. Both P and Q are true, and Q is not the reason for P.

questions.formula_note: Belady's anomaly: FIFO page replacement lacks the stack property, so increasing frames can increase page faults. This is independent of locality of reference.

questions.question:
A process has been allocated 3 page frames. Assume that none of the pages of the process are available in the memory initially. The process makes the following sequence of page references (reference string):
1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 4, 6, 3, 1
If optimal page replacement policy is used, how many page faults occur for the above reference string?
A. 7
B. 8
C. 9
D. 10
questions.to_find: Number of page faults with Optimal page replacement for the given reference string and 3 frames

understand.plain: Optimal page replacement (also called OPT or Belady's optimal algorithm) always replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. We trace through each page reference, checking if the page is in memory (hit) or not (fault). On a fault with full frames, we evict the page whose next use is furthest away.

keywords.explain: Replace the page whose next use is furthest in the future. This gives the minimum possible page faults for any algorithm.
keywords.example: If frames hold pages A(next use: 5), B(next use: 2), C(next use: 10), on a fault evict C.

keywords.explain: Occurs when the referenced page is not currently in any of the allocated frames and must be loaded from disk.
keywords.example: Initially all frames are empty, so the first few accesses are always faults (cold misses).

understand.visual_alt: Table showing frame contents after each reference: faults at positions 1,2,3,4,7,6,1 (7 total) and hits at positions 4(second) and 3(second).

given.aim: Count page faults when Optimal replacement is applied to reference string 1,2,3,4,7,4,6,3,1 with 3 frames.

terms.meaning: Only 3 pages can be in memory at any time.
terms.example: After loading 1,2,3 the frames are full; the next miss must evict one.
terms.connects: Limits how many pages can reside in memory simultaneously.

terms.meaning: The sequence of pages the process accesses.
terms.example: Page 4 appears at position 4 and 6; page 3 at positions 3 and 8.
terms.connects: Used to simulate the page replacement decisions step by step.

terms.meaning: When a fault occurs and all frames are full, evict the page whose next reference is furthest in the future (or never referenced again).
terms.example: If page A is next used at step 8 and page B is next used at step 5, evict A.
terms.connects: Minimizes total page faults — provides a theoretical lower bound.

given.plan: Simulate each access: check if the page is in frames (hit) or not (fault). On a fault with full frames, look ahead in the reference string to find which resident page is used furthest in the future and evict it. Count total faults.

steps.title: Simulate first three accesses (cold misses)
steps.formula_raw: Empty frames => every access is a fault
steps.apply:
Access 1: miss, frames=[1], faults=1
Access 2: miss, frames=[1,2], faults=2
Access 3: miss, frames=[1,2,3], faults=3
Frames now full.
steps.note: All three are compulsory (cold) misses since no page is pre-loaded.

steps.title: Simulate remaining accesses with Optimal eviction
steps.formula_raw: Evict page with furthest next use
steps.apply:
Access 4 (page 4): miss. Future of 1@pos9, 2@never, 3@pos8. Evict 2 (never used again). frames=[1,3,4], faults=4
Access 5 (page 7): miss. Future of 1@pos9, 3@pos8, 4@pos6. Evict 1 (used latest). frames=[7,3,4], faults=5
Access 6 (page 4): hit. frames=[7,3,4]
Access 7 (page 6): miss. Future of 7@never, 3@pos8, 4@never. Evict 7 (or 4, both never again; evict 7). frames=[6,3,4], faults=6
Access 8 (page 3): hit. frames=[6,3,4]
Access 9 (page 1): miss. Future of 6@never, 3@never, 4@never. Evict any (say 6). frames=[1,3,4], faults=7
steps.note: Hits at positions 6 (page 4) and 8 (page 3); all other accesses are faults. Total = 7.

questions.formula_note: Optimal (OPT/Belady's optimal) page replacement: evict the page whose next use is furthest in the future, guaranteeing minimum page faults.

questions.question:
A process has been allocated 3 page frames. Assume that none of the pages of the process are available in the memory initially. The process makes the following sequence of page references (reference string):
1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 4, 6, 3, 1
Least Recently Used (LRU) page replacement policy is a practical approximation to optimal page replacement. For the above reference string, how many more page faults occur with LRU than with the optimal page replacement policy?
A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. 3
questions.to_find: Extra page faults with LRU compared to Optimal for reference string 1,2,3,4,7,4,6,3,1 with 3 frames

understand.plain: LRU evicts the page that was least recently used (the one not accessed for the longest time). Optimal evicts the page not used for the longest time in the future. We already know Optimal gives 7 faults; we now simulate LRU to find its fault count, then subtract to get the difference.

keywords.explain: On a page fault, evict the page that was accessed least recently (furthest in the past).
keywords.example: If pages A,B,C are in frames and last accessed at times 5,3,8 respectively, LRU evicts B (accessed earliest).

keywords.explain: LRU uses past access history as a proxy for future access patterns, typically performing close to Optimal in practice.
keywords.example: Under working-set locality, LRU keeps recently used (likely soon reused) pages in memory.

understand.visual_alt: LRU trace for 1,2,3,4,7,4,6,3,1 with 3 frames yields 8 faults vs Optimal's 7 — one extra fault at position 8 (page 3).

given.aim: Find how many more page faults LRU produces compared to Optimal on reference string 1,2,3,4,7,4,6,3,1 with 3 frames.

terms.meaning: Memory can hold at most 3 pages simultaneously.
terms.example: After 3 cold misses, every subsequent access either hits or evicts one page.
terms.connects: Same constraint as in 7.16.14 for direct comparison.

terms.meaning: From the previous question, Optimal produces 7 faults on this reference string.
terms.connects: Baseline for computing the extra faults with LRU.

terms.meaning: Evict the page not accessed for the longest elapsed time.
terms.example: Access sequence ...A...B...C... page A is evicted first on a fault.
terms.connects: Different from Optimal's future-looking strategy, may lead to more faults.

given.plan: Simulate LRU on the reference string with 3 frames, track recency order, count faults, then subtract 7 (Optimal faults).

steps.title: Simulate LRU page replacement
steps.formula_raw: Evict page with oldest last-use time
steps.apply:
Access 1: miss, frames=[1], recency:1, faults=1
Access 2: miss, frames=[1,2], recency:1<2, faults=2
Access 3: miss, frames=[1,2,3], recency:1<2<3, faults=3
Access 4: miss, LRU=1 (oldest). Evict 1. frames=[2,3,4], recency:2<3<4, faults=4
Access 7: miss, LRU=2. Evict 2. frames=[3,4,7], recency:3<4<7, faults=5
Access 4: hit. frames=[3,7,4], recency:3<7<4(refreshed), faults=5
Access 6: miss, LRU=3. Evict 3. frames=[7,4,6], recency:7<4<6, faults=6
Access 3: miss, LRU=7. Evict 7. frames=[4,6,3], recency:4<6<3, faults=7
Access 1: miss, LRU=4. Evict 4. frames=[6,3,1], faults=8
steps.note: LRU produces 8 page faults: hits only at position 6 (page 4). Optimal had hits at positions 6 and 8.

steps.title: Compute the difference
steps.formula_raw: Extra faults = LRU faults - Optimal faults
steps.apply:
LRU faults = 8
Optimal faults = 7
Extra faults = 8 - 7 = 1
steps.note: The single extra fault occurs at position 8 when LRU evicts page 7 (instead of keeping 3 as Optimal does).

solution.result: LRU has 1 more page fault than Optimal. Answer: B. 1

questions.formula_note: LRU approximates Optimal by using past access history as a predictor of future access; extra faults arise when the least recently used page is actually needed sooner than a different resident page.

questions.question:
In which one of the following page replacement policies, Belady's anomaly may occur?
A. FIFO
B. Optimal
C. LRU
D. MRU
questions.to_find: Which page replacement policy can exhibit Belady's anomaly

understand.plain: Belady's anomaly is a counterintuitive situation where increasing the number of page frames allocated to a process results in more page faults rather than fewer. This anomaly can only occur in page replacement algorithms that do not have the 'stack property'. FIFO is the classic example, while LRU and Optimal are stack algorithms and immune to this anomaly.

keywords.explain: A phenomenon where more memory frames lead to more page faults, observed in FIFO page replacement.
keywords.example: Reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5: 3 frames -> 9 faults, 4 frames -> 10 faults under FIFO.

keywords.explain: An algorithm has the stack property if the set of pages in memory with n frames is always a subset of the pages in memory with n+1 frames. Stack algorithms cannot exhibit Belady's anomaly.
keywords.example: LRU and Optimal have the stack property; FIFO does not.

understand.visual_alt: Only FIFO among the choices lacks the stack property, making it susceptible to Belady's anomaly. LRU, Optimal, and MRU are stack-based algorithms immune to this anomaly.

given.aim: Identify the page replacement policy among FIFO, Optimal, LRU, and MRU that is susceptible to Belady's anomaly.

terms.meaning: Evicts the page that has been in memory the longest, regardless of usage.
terms.example: If pages 1,2,3 are loaded in that order, next fault evicts page 1.
terms.connects: Does not have the stack property; susceptible to Belady's anomaly.

terms.meaning: Evicts the page whose next use is furthest in the future.
terms.example: Always makes the theoretically best eviction choice.
terms.connects: Has the stack property; immune to Belady's anomaly.

terms.meaning: Evicts the page not accessed for the longest time in the past.
terms.example: Access history ...2...1...3...: evict 2 on a fault.
terms.connects: Has the stack property; immune to Belady's anomaly.

given.plan: Recall that Belady's anomaly occurs only in algorithms that lack the stack (inclusion) property. Among the options, FIFO is the well-known algorithm without the stack property, while LRU and Optimal are stack algorithms.

steps.title: Identify algorithms with and without the stack property
steps.formula_raw: Stack property: S(n) subset_of S(n+1) for all n
steps.apply:
LRU: has stack property (the n pages in memory with n frames are always a subset of n+1 frames). Immune.
Optimal: has stack property. Immune.
MRU: has stack property. Immune.
FIFO: does NOT have the stack property — the resident set with n frames is not guaranteed to be a subset of the resident set with n+1 frames.
steps.note: The stack property is the key mathematical condition that prevents Belady's anomaly.

steps.title: Conclude which policy exhibits the anomaly
steps.apply: Since only FIFO lacks the stack property among the given options, Belady's anomaly may occur only with FIFO. A concrete example: reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 gives 9 faults with 3 frames and 10 faults with 4 frames under FIFO.
steps.note: The question asks 'may occur', confirming FIFO is the answer.

questions.formula_note: Stack property of page replacement algorithms: LRU and Optimal satisfy it (immune to Belady's anomaly); FIFO does not (susceptible to Belady's anomaly).

questions.question:
A system uses FIFO page replacement policy. It has 4 page frames with no pages loaded to begin with. The system first accesses 100 distinct pages in some order and then accesses the same 100 pages but now in the reverse order. How many page faults will occur?
A. 196
B. 192
C. 197
D. 195
questions.to_find: Total page faults when 100 distinct pages are accessed in order then in reverse order, using FIFO with 4 frames

understand.plain: With 4 frames and 100 distinct pages accessed in order, there are 100 cold-start faults. After this, the 4 frames hold the last 4 pages loaded (pages 97, 98, 99, 100 in FIFO order). When the reverse access begins (100, 99, ..., 1), the first 4 accesses (100, 99, 98, 97) are hits. From page 96 downward, every access is a miss because those pages were evicted during the first pass.

keywords.explain: Pages are kept in a queue; the page at the front (oldest loaded) is evicted on a miss.
keywords.example: Load 1,2,3,4 into 4 frames: queue=[1,2,3,4]. Next miss evicts 1.

keywords.explain: The first access to any page always causes a fault since it has never been loaded.
keywords.example: With all frames empty, accesses to 10 new distinct pages cause 10 cold misses.

understand.visual_alt: After the first forward pass of 100 pages, frames contain pages {97,98,99,100}. Reverse pass: pages 100,99,98,97 hit; pages 96,95,...,1 each miss (96 faults). Total = 100 + 96 = 196.

given.aim: Count total page faults for forward access (100 distinct pages) followed by reverse access of the same pages, with 4 frames and FIFO.

terms.meaning: Memory can hold 4 pages; all frames start empty causing compulsory misses.
terms.example: First 4 accesses are guaranteed faults regardless of policy.
terms.connects: Determines which pages remain after the first 100 accesses.

terms.meaning: Pages p_1, p_2, ..., p_100 accessed sequentially, each new (no repeats).
terms.example: Access 1,2,3,...,100: every access is a cold miss = 100 faults.
terms.connects: Establishes the FIFO queue state before the reverse pass starts.

terms.term: State of frames after forward pass
terms.meaning: With FIFO and 4 frames after loading pages 1 through 100 sequentially, frames hold pages 97, 98, 99, 100 (the last 4 loaded).
terms.example: FIFO queue at end of forward pass: [97, 98, 99, 100] with 97 as oldest.
terms.connects: Determines which reverse accesses are hits vs faults.

given.plan: Count faults in the forward pass (all 100 are misses). Determine frame state at end of forward pass. Simulate the reverse pass, identifying how many of the 100 pages are hits (only those still in frames: 97,98,99,100 -> at most 4 hits). Count faults in reverse pass. Add both counts.

steps.title: Forward pass: access pages 1 to 100
steps.formula_raw: All distinct, all cold misses = 100 faults
steps.apply:
Pages 1, 2, ..., 100 are all distinct and none pre-loaded. Every access is a fault. With 4 frames, the FIFO queue after loading all 100 pages holds the last 4: pages 97, 98, 99, 100 (97 being the oldest in the queue).
Faults in forward pass = 100.
steps.note: During the forward pass, no page repeats so no hits occur at all.

steps.title: Reverse pass: access pages 100, 99, ..., 1
steps.apply:
Reverse order: 100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95, ..., 1.
Access 100: hit (in frames). Access 99: hit. Access 98: hit. Access 97: hit.
Access 96: miss. FIFO evicts 97 (oldest). frames=[98,99,100,96].
Access 95: miss. FIFO evicts 98. frames=[99,100,96,95].
... continuing, each page from 96 down to 1 is a miss (96 total faults in this segment).
Faults in reverse pass = 96.
steps.note: Only the last 4 pages loaded in the forward pass (97,98,99,100) are still in frames at the start of the reverse pass, giving exactly 4 hits and 96 misses.

questions.formula_note: FIFO page replacement: after a forward pass of N distinct pages with F frames, exactly F pages remain (the last F accessed). The reverse pass produces N-F faults (only the last F pages hit). Total = N + (N - F) = 2N - F = 200 - 4 = 196.
